did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Transfer Learning with a Tuned EfficientNetV2L classifier to predict skin diseases. It employs data augmentation techniques on the HAM10000 dataset of dermatoscopic images to balance class distribution and improve the performance of the deep learning model for skin lesion classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from two specific experiments evaluating a Tuned EfficientNetV2L classifier on the HAM10000 dataset using data augmentation. It includes a methodology section, experimental setup, performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall), and a confusion matrix, which are characteristic of an original research study rather than a review. Although published in a conference proceedings (IRASET), it reports new empirical findings.

title: Data Balancing through Data Augmentation to Improve Transfer Learning Performance for Skin Disease Prediction
journal: 2023 3rd International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
brief_summary: This study addresses class imbalance in skin disease prediction by evaluating data augmentation techniques to improve transfer learning performance. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dataset of dermatoscopic images and applied a Tuned EfficientNetV2L classifier. Two experiments were conducted: one with a default unbalanced dataset structure and another using data augmentation to balance the class distribution. Performance was assessed using accuracy, precision, and recall metrics. The results demonstrated that balancing the dataset through augmentation significantly improved model performance, achieving a 6% increase in accuracy compared to the baseline unbalanced model.

lesion_types_studied.details: Class 0 in the HAM10000 dataset; used in classification task with class weight 5.0.
Class 1 in the HAM10000 dataset; used in classification task with class weight 5.0.
Class 2 in the HAM10000 dataset; used in classification task with class weight 3.0.
Class 3 in the HAM10000 dataset; used in classification task with class weight 8.0; noted as the least frequent class.
Class 4 in the HAM10000 dataset; used in classification task with class weight 3.0.
Class 5 in the HAM10000 dataset; used in classification task with class weight 1.0; noted as the most prevalent class.
Class 6 in the HAM10000 dataset; used in classification task with class weight 8.0.

all_task_types.target: Skin disease diagnostic categories (Actinic keratosis, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper evaluates a Tuned EfficientNetV2L classifier for multi-class skin disease prediction using the HAM10000 dataset. The task involves assigning discrete diagnostic labels to dermatoscopic images. Performance was assessed using accuracy, precision, recall (sensitivity), specificity, and confusion matrices, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive dataset of dermatoscopic images of skin lesions, gathered from over 7000 anonymous patients. It contains over 10,000 labeled images and is annotated with corresponding diagnoses.

use.training: Used for training the baseline and tuned EfficientNetV2L classifier. The paper notes training on 80% of instances in one context and 25% in another, indicating inconsistent reporting or different experimental splits, but explicitly states the model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset.
use.validation: Used for validation via cross-validation method and plotting learning/validation curves to check for over/underfitting.
use.testing: Used for testing the model performance. The paper states testing on 25% of the HAM10000 dataset in one section, though this conflicts with the 80% training figure mentioned nearby; the dataset served as the source for the test set.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the baseline and tuned transfer learning models.
To assess model performance by calculating True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, and False Negatives for each class.
To check for overfitting or underfitting by plotting accuracy and loss against epochs.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance improvements were described qualitatively (e.g., 'improves the performance', 'enhanced') and quantitatively using accuracy, precision, recall, and loss metrics without statistical significance testing.

Experiment 1 (Baseline Model / Unbalance.notes: The model was trained on 80% instances and tested on 25% of the HAM10000 dataset. The highest accuracy was around 91%. The confusion matrix (Figure 4) indicated that the model was able to accurately predict positive cases (TP) but also predicted some cases incorrectly (FP). The model was able to accurately predict negative cases (TN) but failed to predict some positive cases (FN).

Experiment 2 (Fine-tuned Model / Balance.notes: The EfficientNetV2 pre-trained model was fine-tuned on the augmented dataset. The accuracy is higher than the baseline model, and the loss is lower. The classification performance is enhanced compared to the baseline experiment. The learning and validation curves showed no over or under fitting.

architecture_details.scaling_method: Scaling method and neural architecture search algorithm
architecture_details.parameter_efficiency: Designed to improve parameter efficiency and training speed

training_details.training_schedule: Fine-tuned on augmented dataset; Baseline model trained on default dataset

classification_or_output_head.details: Multi-class classification for 7 skin disease categories

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: Rotation (25 degrees), Shifting width and height (15%), Shearing (15%), Flipping (horizontal and vertical), Brightness change (range [0.8 : 1.2])

Class weighting: Application of class weights to balance the dataset during training (Actinic keratoses: 5.0, Basal cell carcinoma: 5.0, Benign keratosis-like lesions: 3.0, Dermatofibroma: 8.0, Melanoma: 3.0, Melanocytic nevi: 1.0, Vascular lesions: 8.0)
Encoding: Conversion of categorical variables to integers
Deletion: Removal of missing data and extraneous features

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Width and height shifting applied at 15%.

Used to balance the dataset during training by assigning weights to classes: Actinic keratoses (5.0), Basal cell carcinoma (5.0), Benign keratosis-like lesions (3.0), Dermatofibroma (8.0), Melanoma (3.0), Melanocytic nevi (1.0), and Vascular lesions (8.0).

conclusions: Data balancing through data augmentation is a crucial step in improving the generalization and performance of transfer learning models for skin disease prediction, particularly when dealing with rare diseases and limited minority class samples.
The proposed methodology using a Tuned EfficientNetV2L classifier on a balanced HAM10000 dataset achieved a 6% improvement in accuracy (97.65%) compared to the baseline unbalanced model (91.87%).

Balancing the dataset via data augmentation significantly enhanced classification performance, resulting in higher precision (80.09% vs 49.98%), sensitivity (98.23% vs 93.66%), and specificity (82.23% vs 44.37%), while reducing loss (4.32% vs 9.03%).

The balanced model demonstrated improved ability to correctly predict positive cases and reduced incorrect predictions compared to the baseline, indicating that data augmentation effectively mitigates class imbalance issues.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the long training time caused by the boosting algorithm and large number of features, and notes that data augmentation techniques like flipping or rotating may not be representative of real-world data or suitable for all data types (e.g., time series).

Future work will involve combining data augmentation with other techniques such as feature selection or fine-tuning pre-trained models to further improve performance, and testing the approach on other medical imaging datasets and diverse skin types.
important_remarks: The paper reports conflicting data split proportions, stating the model was trained on 80% of instances in one context and 25% in another, which raises concerns about experimental reproducibility and potential data leakage or inconsistent reporting.

The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the HAM10000 dataset; the authors acknowledge that future work is needed to test the approach on diverse skin types and other medical imaging datasets.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance improvements were described qualitatively and quantitatively without statistical significance testing.

The paper acknowledges that data augmentation techniques like flipping or rotating may not be representative of real-world data or suitable for all data types, such as time series, limiting the generalizability of the caveats.
The study notes a limitation regarding the long training time caused by the boosting algorithm and the large number of features, which may impact deployment efficiency.

The baseline model showed high accuracy (91.87%) but low specificity (44.37%) and precision (49.98%), indicating a strong bias towards the majority class, which highlights the critical need for the reported balancing techniques.
